text.title: Channel Research Five-Step Process
text.summary: Customer discovery mapping, channel fit matrix scoring, economics modeling, micro-test framework, and pre-registered kill criteria.

rule.statement: Use a free-text "How did you hear about us?" field, not a dropdown — dropdowns force respondents into the wrong bucket and corrupt the discovery map.
rule.rationale: Dropdowns create selection bias toward the channels you already know. Free text surfaces channels you did not consider and catches "dark social" attribution (podcast, friend DM, Slack community).

list.item: Customer interviews: "How did you find [competitor]?" and "Where do you usually discover new tools?"
Analytics: UTM source breakdown, referrer analysis (multi-touch, not last-click)
Competitor analysis: SimilarWeb traffic mix, Meta Ad Library, LinkedIn Ad Library
Audience research: SparkToro for "where does this audience hang out?" — sites, podcasts, social handles

section.title: Step 2: Channel Fit Matrix

rule.statement: Score each channel on six factors (audience presence 25%, competitors using 15%, cost to test 20%, time to results 15%, scalability 15%, team capability 10%) and require a cited source or interview reference for audience presence and competitors-using scores.
rule.rationale: Uncited scores are preferences disguised as analysis. Forcing citations for the two highest-weight factors prevents agents from recommending SEO by default because training data is saturated with SEO examples.

section.p: Viable channels: those scoring above 3.5/5 weighted total AND with LTV:CAC > 3:1 in Phase-2 testing.

list.item: Cost per impression → CTR → CPC → conversion rate → CAC
LTV:CAC ratio target: > 3:1 for Phase-2 scaling, > 1:1 minimum for Phase-1 micro-tests
Time to ROI: organic channels typically 6-12 months; paid channels immediate but front-loaded cost

rule.statement: Treat LTV:CAC > 3:1 as a Phase-2 gate, not a Phase-1 gate — Phase-1 micro-tests are buying signal, not unit economics validation.
rule.rationale: LTV is uncertain for early-stage products. Killing a channel after a $300 test because LTV:CAC is 2:1 is premature — the sample size is too small for economic conclusions.

rule.statement: Predefine a kill criterion (e.g., "if CAC > $X after $500 spend, kill") before money is spent — prevents sunk-cost continuation.
rule.rationale: Post-hoc evaluation always finds reasons to continue. A pre-registered kill criterion removes the sunk-cost bias and makes test results actionable regardless of the outcome.

list.item: Phase 1 micro-test: $100-500 budget, 1-2 weeks, answer "does this channel generate signal?"
Phase 2 scale test: $1-5K budget, 1 month, answer "what are the economics?"
Phase 3 optimize: tune targeting/creative before increasing budget
Separate "channel" from "creative": 3 distinct creative concepts before killing a channel

text.reference: Weighted matrix scorer from YAML channel definitions.
